subject: ap-government
theme: Americans' political beliefs are shaped by political socialization, and the patterns of public opinion — measured through polling — reveal systematic ideological divisions along economic, social, and identity lines that parties and candidates must navigate.
core_concepts: Political ideology: a set of beliefs about government's proper role — American political culture clusters around liberalism (government should promote equality and expand social programs) and conservatism (government should limit regulation, promote individual responsibility, reduce taxes); libertarianism and authoritarianism represent additional dimensions.

Political socialization — the process by which citizens acquire political beliefs: the most powerful agents are family (strongest predictor of party identification), then schools (civic education), peers (opinion reinforcement), mass media (agenda-setting, framing, priming), and major life events (economic crises, wars shift opinion cohorts).

Public opinion and polling methodology: random sampling, adequate sample size, question wording, and response rate all affect validity; margin of error measures sampling uncertainty; push polls are designed to change opinion rather than measure it; exit polls use samples of actual voters on election day.

Party identification: the most stable predictor of vote choice; most Americans identify as Democrat, Republican, or Independent; dealignment (declining partisan attachment) and growing independent identification are documented trends since the 1950s — but most 'independents' consistently lean toward one party.

Ideological divisions: the gender gap (women more Democratic on average), racial gap, education gap (college graduates more Democratic in recent elections), age gap (older voters more conservative), and income gap all produce systematic differences in party identification and policy preferences.

Political culture — the broadly shared values underlying American politics: individualism, free enterprise, rule of law, and equality of opportunity command broad consensus; equality of outcome and government economic intervention are more contested; these cultural foundations constrain what policies are politically viable.

Measuring public opinion: approval ratings track presidential performance over time; ideological self-identification surveys show about 35-40% conservative, 20-25% liberal, 35-40% moderate; operational-symbolic inconsistency — many Americans are symbolically conservative but operationally liberal (preferring specific programs they oppose in principle).

Realignment vs. dealignment: realignment is large-scale movement of voting blocs from one party to another (1932 New Deal realignment; 1960s-80s South shifting Republican); dealignment is declining partisan attachment without clear movement to the other party — both patterns shape contemporary political competition.
exam_tip: AP exam questions on Unit 4 frequently use polling data tables or charts as stimulus — practice identifying sampling errors, question wording effects, and demographic breakdowns; know the specific demographic groups (gender, education, race, income) that systematically differ in party identification and ideology.
